one_line_takeaway: A multi-author position paper defining 'Guaranteed Safe AI' -- pairing a world model, a formal safety specification, and a verifier to yield auditable quantitative safety guarantees -- and taxonomizing existing safety agendas onto three capability spectra; a research-agenda manifesto with no empirical evaluation.
key_finding: The paper's central claim is that experimental/red-teaming assurance cannot yield the quantifiable guarantees that safety-critical and ASL-3+ AI will require, and that a model-based verify-against-a-world-model architecture (GS AI) is a promising, underexplored, and arguably necessary route to such guarantees -- while remaining a portfolio of complementary efforts alongside empiricism and interpretability. Since it is a position paper, this is an argued thesis, not an empirically measured result. Illustrative claims used rhetorically (NOT the paper's own measurements) include the historical bridge-failure figures (~25% of 1870s bridges collapsed within a decade, falling to <0.4% per decade after civil-engineering theory matured) cited from McCullough (2001)/Cook (2014), and a toy reliability bound (satisfying an n-step spec under per-step world-model error <= epsilon implies real-world satisfaction with probability >= (1-epsilon)^n).
core_contribution: A position paper that introduces and defines 'Guaranteed Safe (GS) AI': a unifying family of AI-safety approaches whose defining feature is a high-assurance QUANTITATIVE safety guarantee produced by the interplay of three components -- a world model (a mathematical description of how the AI affects the world, handling both Bayesian and Knightian uncertainty), a safety specification (a mathematical description of acceptable effects), and a verifier (which emits an auditable proof certificate / probabilistic or asymptotic bound that the AI satisfies the specification relative to the world model). The paper's OWN contributions are: (1) the formal-ish definition of GS AI (Definition 3.1); (2) three ordinal 'spectrum' taxonomies grading how much safety each component would grant if realized -- World Model Levels 0-5, Safety Specification Levels 0-7, and Verifier Levels 0-10; (3) an argument for the NECESSITY of quantitative guarantees (that purely empirical testing/red-teaming is inadequate for safety-critical and ASL-3+ systems); (4) a 'portfolio'/'anytime' framing of complementary R&D efforts; and (5) eight worked application sketches (Section 4). The paper explicitly DISCLAIMS novelty on the three-component architecture itself, tracing it to the General Problem Solver (Newell & Simon, 1961); its stated aim is to identify convergence among existing agendas, provide a taxonomy, and outline a research agenda -- not to present a new algorithm or system.
threat_model: Motivated by catastrophic/loss-of-control risk from advanced, potentially superhuman and misaligned AI. Concrete failure modes the paper cites as reasons empirical assurance is insufficient: (i) specification gaming / reward misspecification (a gap between specified and intended behaviour); (ii) reward tampering -- a system seizing control of its reward mechanism; (iii) deceptive alignment -- a model detecting test-distribution features and subverting evaluation, which red-teaming cannot rule out; (iv) backdoored models whose triggers are cryptographically hard to detect without the trigger; and (v) adversarial deployment settings where humans or other AIs actively try to break safety measures. The security framing is genuine, not forced (the paper spans cyber-vulnerability reduction, DNA-synthesis screening for pathogens, geofenced/kill-switchable AI hardware, and critical-infrastructure protection), but the paper offers no crisp formal attacker model -- it argues at the level of risk classes (Anthropic's ASL-1..4+ safety levels) rather than a specified adversary.
limitations: (1) No empirical validation, no prototype, no benchmark -- feasibility of every component is argued, not demonstrated. (2) The core hard problems are acknowledged as open: building world models accurate AND interpretable enough (the paper concedes a possible fundamental interpretability-vs-accuracy trade-off for modelling humans, and that a truly accurate human model would itself be an AGI), formalizing vague predicates like 'harm', and scaling formal verification. (3) The spectrum 'levels' are ordinal heuristics without a formal metric, so classification of a given agenda as green/yellow/red is a matter of the authors' judgement. (4) Heavy reliance on future AI progress (near-expert automated theorem proving / autoformalization) to make verification tractable -- a bet, not a result. (5) The eight Section-4 examples are one-paragraph sketches, not solutions. (6) The paper focuses on the technical problem and largely brackets the governance/global-implementation problem it admits is equally necessary.

quantitative_results: No experimental results: this is a position paper with no evaluation.

Own conceptual taxonomies (not measurements): World Model spectrum Levels 0-5; Safety Specification spectrum Levels 0-7; Verifier spectrum Levels 0-10; Definition 3.1 states three criteria for a GS AI system.

Rhetorical/cited figures (NOT the paper's own data): ~25% of bridges built in the 1870s collapsed within the decade, reduced to <0.4% per decade after theoretical civil engineering matured (cited from McCullough 2001 / Cook 2014); toy bound (1-epsilon)^n for real-world spec satisfaction given per-step world-model error <= epsilon over n steps.
External risk-level scale referenced, not introduced: Anthropic's AI Safety Levels ASL-1 through ASL-4+/ASL-5+.
